title: list
description: A doubly linked list container: no [], fast insertion/removal anywhere, and the vector trade-off finally made concrete.
objectives: Understand list as a doubly linked list with no direct indexing
Use push_front(), push_back(), pop_front(), and pop_back()
Traverse a list with an iterator, since [] isn't available
Compare list and vector's trade-offs directly

sections.text: STL Iterators said: "a list has no meaningful way to jump straight to element 5 without walking through the ones before it." Let's see exactly why, and what list is actually good at instead.
list is a doubly linked list — a sequence of elements where each one knows only its neighbors, not a fixed position in contiguous memory the way a vector's elements do.

Because there's no contiguous block of memory, there's no way to jump directly to "the 5th element" the way array indexing does — reaching it means following links from the front (or back), one element at a time.

vector only had push_back()/pop_back(); adding to the front of a vector requires shifting every existing element over, an expensive operation. list's linked structure makes push_front()/pop_front() just as cheap as the back operations.

numbers[1] would be a compile-time error — list simply has no operator[] at all. This is exactly the begin()/end()/*it/it++ pattern from STL Iterators, and it's the only way to walk through a list's elements.

Once you already have an iterator pointing at some position, list.insert(it, value) or list.erase(it) only needs to relink a couple of neighboring connections — it never has to shift other elements over, unlike vector's insert()/erase(), which may need to move every element after the insertion point.

rows: No indexing at all — only sequential access via iterators
Fast push_back(); push_front() is expensive (shifts everything)
push_front() and push_back() are both cheap
insert()/erase() in the middle can be expensive (shifts elements)
insert()/erase() at a known position is cheap (just relinks)
Elements scattered in memory — less cache-friendly

sections.text: There's no universally "better" one — vector wins when indexing and iterating in order dominate; list wins when frequent insertion/removal at arbitrary positions dominates and indexing isn't needed.

sections.title: A treasure hunt chain vs numbered parking spots
sections.text: Numbered parking spots (vector) let you walk straight to spot #47 without visiting the others. A treasure hunt (list), where each clue only tells you where the next clue is, has no such shortcut — you must follow the chain from the start. But inserting a brand-new clue into the middle of a treasure hunt is trivial (just point the previous clue somewhere new); inserting a new numbered spot into the middle of a parking lot means renumbering everything after it.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Trying to use [] on a list — it doesn't exist; use iterators instead.
Choosing list when frequent indexed access is actually needed — every indexed access degrades to a slow sequential walk.
Assuming list is always 'better' because insertion is cheap — vector is usually faster overall for typical DSA use, thanks to contiguous memory and simpler operations.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What kind of data structure is list?
questions.answer: A doubly linked list, where each element only knows its neighbors rather than occupying a fixed position in contiguous memory.

questions.question: Why doesn't list support the [] operator?
questions.answer: Because reaching a specific position requires following links from the front or back one element at a time — there's no contiguous memory to jump into directly, unlike vector.

questions.question: Why is insertion/removal at a known position generally cheaper in a list than in a vector?
questions.answer: A list only needs to relink a couple of neighboring connections, while a vector may need to shift every element after the insertion or removal point.

questions.question: When would list be preferred over vector?
questions.answer: When frequent insertion or removal happens at arbitrary positions and direct indexed access isn't needed — vector remains the default otherwise.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: list is a doubly linked list, where each element knows only its neighbors rather than sitting in contiguous memory like vector's elements do — this is exactly why it has no [] operator, and why traversal requires iterators via begin()/end(). Its strength is cheap insertion and removal at any known position, including the front with push_front()/pop_front(), since it only needs to relink neighboring connections rather than shift elements the way vector's insert()/erase() sometimes must. The trade-off is no direct indexing and generally worse cache performance, since elements aren't stored together in memory.

sections.title: Trick to Remember list
sections.text: list = a treasure hunt chain, follow the clues, no jumping ahead. Cheap to insert a new clue anywhere; no shortcuts to a specific position.
